payload.text:
Professional Project Prompt: Thiia AI Development
Project Overview
Build Thiia AI - a browser-based AI-powered development environment combining code execution, AI chat assistance, and research capabilities.
Tech Stack & Services
Frontend
Framework: Next.js 14+ (App Router)
Styling: Tailwind CSS
Code Editor: Monaco Editor
Deployment: Vercel
Backend & Services
Database: Supabase (PostgreSQL)
Authentication: Clerk
AI Models:
Gemini 2.0 Flash - Code generation & quick responses
Claude Sonnet 4.5 - Complex coding tasks
ChatGPT-4 - Research & conversational AI
API Configuration
# Clerk Authentication
NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY=pk_test_dW5pZmllZC1qYWNrYWwtNzMuY2xlcmsuYWNjb3VudHMuZGV2JA
CLERK_SECRET_KEY=sk_test_6D6ikKz0SyknDksyXADot6tqLW4FPpkkROJ8cPmqJ5

# Supabase
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://dwjzocaxdcngmmmbejst.supabase.co
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJpc3MiOiJzdXBhYmFzZSIsInJlZiI6ImR3anpvY2F4ZGNuZ21tbWJlanN0Iiwicm9sZSI6ImFub24iLCJpYXQiOjE3Njk5MzkxMjQsImV4cCI6MjA4NTUxNTEyNH0.uzc7oW6Yh6Yq0UsIvv4mDXJrzkd0OeFQ8PaimFJecZs

# AI APIs
GEMINI_API_KEY=AIzaSyA8bLCDBk6O56kCkl9ySXD5-oKKcbElw4c
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=MK_h1tmjboFseTK9EETPOHzD64aTN-z0kLipTZcu3_c
OPENAI_API_KEY=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
Core Features Specification
1. Authentication System (Clerk)
Sign up / Sign in (Email, Google, GitHub)
Protected routes for authenticated users
User profile management
Session persistence
2. Code Editor Interface
Monaco Editor integration with multi-language support
Syntax highlighting (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, HTML, CSS)
Code execution environment (sandboxed)
File tree navigation (create, rename, delete files/folders)
Split-pane layout: Editor | Console | AI Chat
3. AI Assistant Modes
Mode A: Coding Assistant (Gemini 2.0 Flash + Claude Sonnet 4.5)
Primary: Gemini 2.0 Flash for fast code completion
Fallback: Claude Sonnet 4.5 for complex debugging
Features:
Code explanation
Bug detection & fixes
Code generation from prompts
Refactoring suggestions
Auto-completion inline suggestions
Mode B: Research Assistant (ChatGPT-4)
Web search integration
Documentation lookup
Concept explanations
Learning resources recommendations
Step-by-step tutorials
4. Project Management (Supabase)
Database Schema:
-- Users table (synced with Clerk)
CREATE TABLE users (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  clerk_id TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  email TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Projects table
CREATE TABLE projects (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  description TEXT,
  language TEXT DEFAULT 'javascript',
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Files table
CREATE TABLE files (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  project_id UUID REFERENCES projects(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  path TEXT NOT NULL,
  content TEXT,
  language TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Chat history
CREATE TABLE chat_messages (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  project_id UUID REFERENCES projects(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  role TEXT CHECK (role IN ('user', 'assistant')),
  content TEXT NOT NULL,
  model TEXT, -- 'gemini', 'claude', 'chatgpt'
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Code executions log
CREATE TABLE executions (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  project_id UUID REFERENCES projects(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  code TEXT NOT NULL,
  output TEXT,
  error TEXT,
  language TEXT,
  executed_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
5. Code Execution Engine
Serverless function (Vercel Edge Function or API Route)
Language support: Python, Node.js, JavaScript
Timeout: 30 seconds max
Memory limit: 256MB
Return stdout, stderr, execution time
Application Architecture
Frontend Structure
/app
  /(auth)
    /sign-in
    /sign-up
  /(dashboard)
    /projects
    /editor/[projectId]
  /api
    /execute-code
    /ai/gemini
    /ai/claude
    /ai/chatgpt
/components
  /editor
    - MonacoEditor.tsx
    - FileTree.tsx
    - Console.tsx
  /ai
    - ChatInterface.tsx
    - AISelector.tsx
  /ui (shadcn components)
/lib
  - supabase.ts
  - ai-clients.ts
  - clerk.ts
API Routes
/api/execute-code
POST /api/execute-code
{
  "language": "python",
  "code": "print('Hello World')",
  "projectId": "uuid"
}

Response:
{
  "output": "Hello World\n",
  "error": null,
  "executionTime": "45ms"
}
/api/ai/gemini
POST /api/ai/gemini
{
  "prompt": "Explain this code",
  "code": "const x = 5;",
  "mode": "explain" | "generate" | "debug"
}
/api/ai/claude
POST /api/ai/claude
{
  "messages": [...],
  "task": "complex-refactor"
}
/api/ai/chatgpt
POST /api/ai/chatgpt
{
  "query": "How does React useState work?",
  "context": "research"
}
Development Phases
Phase 1: Foundation (Week 1-2)
[ ] Setup Next.js project with TypeScript
[ ] Configure Clerk authentication
[ ] Setup Supabase database & tables
[ ] Create basic UI layout with Tailwind
[ ] Implement Monaco Editor integration
[ ] Deploy to Vercel (staging)
Phase 2: Core Features (Week 3-4)
[ ] Build file management system
[ ] Create project CRUD operations
[ ] Implement code execution API
[ ] Add Gemini API integration for coding
[ ] Build chat interface
[ ] User dashboard with project list
Phase 3: AI Integration (Week 5-6)
[ ] Integrate Claude Sonnet 4.5 for advanced coding
[ ] Add ChatGPT-4 for research mode
[ ] Implement AI model switching
[ ] Chat history persistence
[ ] Context-aware suggestions
Phase 4: Polish & Launch (Week 7-8)
[ ] Code execution sandboxing improvements
[ ] Error handling & user feedback
[ ] Performance optimization
[ ] Mobile responsive design
[ ] Documentation & onboarding
[ ] Production deployment
Key Features Breakdown
AI Model Selection Logic
function selectAIModel(task: string, complexity: 'low' | 'medium' | 'high') {
  if (task === 'research') return 'chatgpt';
  
  if (complexity === 'low') return 'gemini'; // Fast responses
  if (complexity === 'high') return 'claude'; // Complex reasoning
  
  return 'gemini'; // Default
}
Security Considerations
Row Level Security (RLS) in Supabase
API rate limiting
Code execution timeout & sandboxing
Environment variable protection
Clerk webhook verification
Success Metrics
User can sign up and create project in < 2 minutes
Code execution response time < 3 seconds
AI response time < 5 seconds
99% uptime on Vercel
Support 1000+ concurrent users

**Considering Architecture Choices**

I'm currently focused on evaluating the viability of a browser-based AI development environment, "Thiia AI." While the ideal architecture involves a full-stack (Next.js, Clerk, Supabase, Monaco Editor, Vercel), I'm working to produce a minimal viable product constrained by the current limitations. The immediate challenge involves defining the scope of a useful, single-page application.


**Adapting the Architecture**

I'm adapting my approach to a single-page React application, built with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS, all within a single XML block. Gemini API will handle AI functionality. I'm prioritizing a frontend-only simulation, given the constraints. Backend aspects are being mocked or simplified to maintain a clean architecture and fulfill core visual and functional requirements.


**Simulating Authentication Functionality**

I'm now focusing on Clerk authentication within the frontend-only environment. Given the constraints, I will have to simulate the Auth context as I cannot directly install dependencies. The user provided Clerk keys, but utilizing Clerk React SDK isn't an option. I'll mock the Auth interface to ensure UI functionality without external dependencies. I'm aiming for a robust simulated AuthProvider. The user does not provide keys for a database.


**Simulating Data Persistence**

I'm now focusing on simulating database interactions, as the user is unable to provide an actual Supabase instance. My current plan involves using `localStorage` or in-memory state to handle Projects, Files, and Chat data. This allows for a fully functional demo experience.

**Mocking Code Execution**

I'm now looking at handling real code execution and have decided to mock the execution engine to return "Simulated Output" or use a browser-based `eval` (safely wrapped) for JavaScript. For Python, I will simply mock the execution as a placeholder. This will allow for the app to function without needing a backend sandbox.

**Integrating AI Models**

I'm now prioritizing the Gemini API implementation and will use the `@google/genai` library. I will substitute requests for Claude/ChatGPT with Gemini models, potentially using a "Model Selector" in the UI to route to different system instructions or Gemini model variations.


**Defining Project Structure**

I am now structuring the project for Thiia AI, creating a clear file system to improve the code. The `metadata.json` will store app details, and the `index.html` file will serve as the entry point with a Tailwind CDN. The React root will be in `index.tsx`, with a main router/layout defined in `App.tsx`. I have also defined all necessary interfaces in `types.ts`, and mock data in `constants.ts`.


**Prioritizing Editor Functionality**

I'm now focusing on implementing the editor component as a styled textarea with line numbers, given the constraints of a single-file implementation and no package installation. This ensures immediate functionality without any bundler configuration. I'll mock components for file navigation, console output, and AI chat.


**Implementing AI Interaction**

I'm now focusing on integrating the AI chat features. I'm prioritizing the input area, model selector, and chat history components. I'll utilize Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite for general chat and simple code completion and Gemini 3 Pro for "Deep Reasoning," mirroring the "Claude/Complex" functionality. System instructions will guide the AI as a coding assistant, and I'll include the necessary `process.env.API_KEY` implementation. The code files are being structured to include components and services for the AI chat implementation. I'll split files to maintain reasonable clarity.

entries.description: Entry point with Tailwind CSS and Font Awesome
Gemini API integration with multiple models

payload.text: Integrate a step-by-step debugging interface for JavaScript and Python code within the editor, allowing users to set breakpoints and inspect variables.

**Developing Debugging Interface**

I'm focusing on integrating a step-by-step debugging interface into the `Thiia AI` application. The current setup, with `CodeEditor`, `Console`, and `ChatInterface`, feels basic. I'm exploring enhancements beyond the existing `executeCode` function, which now uses `new Function` for JS, plus a Python interpreter for that.


**Defining Debugging Steps**

I'm now detailing updates for the front-end debugging simulation. The goal is to add breakpoints and variable inspection. I've broken the work into UI and logic components. The plan includes a line-number gutter for the `CodeEditor` and a new `Debugger Panel` to manage execution, and to view variables in a dynamic `Variables View`. I'm starting by mocking out a Monaco-like editor using HTML and CSS for line rendering.


**Simulating Debugging State**

I'm now focusing on a way to simulate a debugger for both JavaScript and Python, given that the application is frontend-focused. For JavaScript, I'm considering wrapping the code in an `async` function and injecting `await checkpoint()` calls at each line, to create simulated "pause points." I need to consider how to handle the Python implementation within this frontend approach.


**Refining Debugging Strategy**

I'm now zeroing in on a debug solution! Parsing variables is too difficult right now, so I'll mock the UI and focus on an "instrumentation" approach. I will split the code by lines, and inject `__debug(lineNum, scope)` before each line. I will have to simulate the python experience using the AI, since I don't have a Python runtime available. Using Gemini to simulate the debug trace seems like a perfect fit, generating the "trace" is ideal for an AI-powered application! I will use a Hybrid approach, with user breakpoints, then code plus breakpoints sent to Gemini, which returns a JSON debug trace.


**Implementing Debugging UI**

I'm now fully committed to the "AI-Powered Debugger" strategy. I'm focusing on UI components: a breakpoint gutter for the `CodeEditor`, a "Debug" button, `DebugControls` (Step, Continue, Stop), and a `VariablesPanel`. State management includes `breakpoints`, `debugMode`, and the AI-generated `debugTrace`, and I now need to implement services to work with Gemini AI. This trace will allow stepping through the AI's execution steps. This approach, using the trace, side steps the interactive "live variable change" in this context.


**Adding Debug State**

I've been working on integrating the new debugging features, including updated interfaces for `DebugState` in `types.ts`. I have added the essential `generateDebugTrace` method to `geminiService.ts`, and am now modifying `App.tsx` for core UI integrations. I am enhancing `CodeEditor` to support line numbers, breakpoints, and UI elements.

I'm now implementing the `CodeEditor`'s HTML structure. I'm wrapping the `textarea` in a relative container with a left-side absolute `gutter` div for line numbers and breakpoint interaction. I'm also adding an absolute, `pointer-events-none` `backdrop` div to highlight the current execution line, making the `textarea` transparent.


**Implementing Gemini Integration**

I'm now implementing the Gemini integration to generate debug traces. I've defined `DebugStep` and `DebugSession` types in `types.ts` and added `getDebugTrace` to `geminiService.ts`. The `CodeEditor` has been refactored to accept `breakpoints`, `onToggleBreakpoint`, and `currentLine` props, and I'm updating `App.tsx` to include `DebuggerPanel`. I'm also considering the syncing of scroll for the editor. To avoid potential issues with `textarea` and `div`, I'm researching using a Monaco-like editor.


**Refining Textarea Strategy**

I'm now determining how to handle the editor with debugging features. Syncing the `textarea` and a separate line number "gutter" looks complicated, but I will try adding padding. The AI-generated trace may be slow or incorrect, but a browser-based interpreter is too complex. My core goal remains: a step-by-step debugging interface for setting breakpoints and variable inspection, and I think it will be better to have the AI simulate this experience. I'll focus on `types.ts`, `geminiService.ts`, and `App.tsx` for these changes.


**Tabulating Output Views**

I'm now integrating a tabbed view within the "Console" to include a "Terminal" and a "Debug Variables" section. I'm also refactoring the `CodeEditor`'s styling, specifically the line height, to precisely match the line numbers in the gutter. The font is set to `font-mono`, with a line height of `text-sm leading-6`. The AI-powered Gemini service needs robust trace generation, with a focus on JSON output for simulation of code execution.

entries.description: Added generateDebugTrace function to simulate execution trace via AI
Implemented Debugger UI with breakpoints, variables panel, and line highlighting. Updated CodeEditor to support gutters and overlays.

payload.text: Implement the 'continue' action in the `ConsoleAndDebugger` component. This action should resume execution from the current step, running through the trace until it hits a breakpoint or the end of the code. Update the `currentLine`, `variables`, and accumulate `output`. If a breakpoint is hit, pause and highlight the new line; if execution finishes, stop debugging.

Add a 'Execution Log' tab to the console area that persistently stores a history of all code executions, including the code snippet, language, output, and execution time, with options to view or clear logs.

**Defining 'Continue' Behavior**

I'm now zeroing in on the 'continue' action within `ConsoleAndDebugger`. My focus is on ensuring it resumes execution smoothly from the present step, updating `currentLine`, `variables`, and accumulating `output` seamlessly until a breakpoint is hit or the process concludes. The crucial step is defining how to actually trigger resumption in the context of the debugging environment.


**Adding Execution Log Feature**

I'm now in the process of adding an "Execution Log" tab to the `ConsoleAndDebugger` component. My plan involves modifying `types.ts` to define the log entry structure, and updating `services/mockDb.ts` to include functions for log management (getting, adding, clearing). I also need to update `App.tsx` to handle the `executionLogs` state, and I need to add state and logic to `App.tsx` to get the debugger to work with my new execution logs.


**Refining 'Continue' Implementation**

I'm revisiting the 'continue' implementation in `handleDebugAction`. The existing `while` loop is present, and I'm ensuring state updates (accumulating output, managing variables, and updating the current line) are performed correctly within and *after* the loop. The breakpoints are properly accounted for, but I want to ensure my updates don't accidentally get lost when jumping. Specifically, I'm examining how to ensure the updates are correctly applied after the continue finishes in the debugger.


**Verifying State Updates**

I'm now zeroing in on verifying the state updates within the 'continue' implementation. I want to ensure my current updates are correctly applied after the loop. Specifically, I'm verifying the current implementation updates state once after the loop. This looks efficient, however, I'm making sure it handles variable updates correctly by taking from the last step executed. I'm also reviewing edge cases related to hitting breakpoints and end-of-trace conditions, to be sure all paths update state correctly.


**Confirming Next Step Accuracy**

I'm now zeroing in on verifying the logic related to breakpoints and the `nextStep` variable. I will be sure it is correct. Also, I have a plan for execution logging, adding a log entry, calling `db.addExecutionLog`, and updating the `executionLogs` state in `executeCode`.

I'm also working on UI updates for the `ConsoleAndDebugger` component: adding a "HISTORY" tab to render the log list, including detail views, and a "Clear" button. I've defined the execution log entry structure in `types.ts`. I have added functions for log management in `services/mockDb.ts`.


**Implementing Log Updates**

I'm now integrating the execution logging features within `App.tsx`. I'm working on how to create the log entry within the `executeCode` function and how to set the start/end times. I need to make sure the state updates are correctly handled when the `activeProject` changes. I also plan to store the entries in local storage.


**Designing Log Display**

I'm now focusing on the UI for displaying execution logs in the `ConsoleAndDebugger` component. I'm adding a "HISTORY" tab that will list each log entry with its timestamp, status (success/error determined by the output), and duration. Each item might show the full output directly or perhaps trigger a detailed view for longer outputs. I'm also considering storing this information as part of the component props, which includes the `executionLogs` and an `onClearLogs` function.


**Validating Loop Logic**

I'm now zeroing in on validating the loop logic within the 'continue' implementation. I want to ensure my index handling within the `while` loop is correct. Specifically, I need to check the exact behaviour of `nextIndex` when a breakpoint is encountered, and ensure the state is correctly updated after we break due to a breakpoint or end of trace. This involves verifying the incrementing and break conditions with the debugger's state. I'm checking the initial and post-execution scenarios.


**Analyzing Breakpoint Behavior**

I'm now analyzing breakpoint behavior within the 'continue' execution. I'm focusing on ensuring proper handling when a breakpoint is encountered *during* a "continue" operation. Specifically, I'm examining scenarios where the next step's line matches the current breakpoint line and avoiding infinite loops or unexpected pauses. I'm focusing on ensuring we iterate and check steps. I will ensure breakpoints are only hit *once* per line.


**Refining Breakpoint Logic**

I'm now refining the logic for breakpoint handling within the 'continue' execution. My plan is to ensure, that even if the debugger is already paused at a breakpoint, resuming should advance by at least one step and then check the current line for a breakpoint. The logic will ensure correct behavior. This will then be implemented into the execution log tab as well.

I'm now writing the code.
I have added the `ExecutionLogEntry` into `types.ts`. I also have added `getExecutionLogs`, `addExecutionLog`, and `clearExecutionLogs` into `services/mockDb.ts`. I have imported `ExecutionLogEntry` and added the `executionLogs` state in `App.tsx`.


**Updating `handleDebugAction`**

I'm now refining the `handleDebugAction` function within `App.tsx`, and I'm updating the 'continue' logic. My current plan is to ensure the `continue` flow works correctly. I need to make sure the loop is correct, and I'm handling the `step` and `continue` scenarios to manage state updates and output accumulation. I'm focusing on breakpoint handling. Specifically, I need to make sure we don't hit the *same* breakpoint that we stopped on.


**Verifying Continue Flow**

I'm now zeroing in on verifying the 'continue' execution flow with breakpoints. I'm focusing on ensuring proper incrementing of `nextIndex` within the loop, and ensuring `nextIndex` is managed correctly to avoid missing or double-hitting steps, especially when breakpoints are involved. I'm reviewing the increment and break conditions in the `while` loop, focusing on getting `nextIndex` and breakpoint handling correct, and making sure the `step` variable's relationship to the index is handled properly. I'm confirming correct step execution and breakpoint management, ensuring the debugger operates as expected.



**Refining Index Management**

I'm now refining how the `nextIndex` is managed within the continue execution flow and handling breakpoints. I'm making certain the incrementing logic within the loop is solid, particularly when we encounter a breakpoint. I need to be sure the debugger moves *past* a breakpoint on continue, unless a breakpoint is hit at the next step, ensuring breakpoints are not skipped or hit multiple times. I'm focusing on the termination of the loop. Specifically, I'm examining scenarios at the end of the trace, and I will be testing these cases.


**Clarifying Execution Index**

I'm now clarifying the relationship between the execution index and state updates, specifically focusing on breakpoints. I'm focusing on clarifying whether the index represents the state *before* or *after* a line is executed. I need to clarify the meaning of variable updates, output, and breakpoints in the context of the trace. Specifically, I'm examining the meaning of "variables" and "output" in the trace entries, and I'll consider how this should be displayed in the debugger.


**Defining Trace Entry Meaning**

I'm now clarifying the relationship between trace entries and debugger state. I'm focusing on the interpretation of `line`, `variables`, and `output` within a trace. I'm reviewing whether each entry represents the state *before* or *after* execution, and how this impacts the debugger's display and behavior. I'm examining the meaning of "variables" and "output" in the trace entries, particularly how this will relate to the line being executed by the debugger.


**Interpreting Trace Entries**

I'm now zeroing in on interpreting trace entries in relation to the debugger's state. I'm confirming that `trace[i]` represents the state *after* the previous step, including the variables and output of a particular line, and that `currentStepIndex` highlights the line to be *executed*. The output is displayed when we *move* to a step and the variables reflect the *result* of the execution. Specifically, in continue, we add output when we skip a step.


**Interpreting Continue Execution**

I'm now zeroing in on interpreting `trace` entries during 'continue' execution. I've confirmed `trace[i]` now represents the state *after* the execution of a line, with `currentStepIndex` pointing to the *next* line to be executed. In the debugger, when we *move* to a step and call `step`, then the previous step is executed, and its output is *added*. With 'continue', the loop adds the accumulated output of *previous* steps.


**Verifying State Accumulation**

I'm now zeroing in on verifying my understanding of trace entries and debugger state during continuous execution. I've focused on ensuring accumulated output correctly reflects the executed steps before a breakpoint or pause, meaning the logic to increment output correctly uses `trace[idx - 1].output` to update `outputAccumulator`. Also, I'm checking startDebugging logic. Specifically, I'm verifying the initial state when the debugger starts.



**Clarifying Step Execution**

I'm now clarifying how step execution works within the debugger and its relationship to the trace entries. I've reevaluated the logic for continue, confirming that `trace[i]` now holds the *result* of the previous step. For stepping, output is displayed when we *move* to a step, but for continue, accumulated output is added during the loop. I'm making sure `outputAccumulator` is updated correctly.


**Refining Continue Logic**

I'm now refining the 'continue' execution logic. My focus is on ensuring `outputAccumulator` is updated correctly within the loop, pulling output from the *previous* step, specifically `trace[idx - 1].output`. Also, I've re-examined the edge case for "End of Program" and will make sure the debugger exits when the last step is hit, confirming the output accumulator updates. Specifically, I'm verifying the initial state when the debugger starts.

entries.description: Implemented 'continue' action in debugger and added Execution Logs tab functionality.

payload.text: Visually highlight the current line of execution in the code editor during debugging sessions.

**Analyzing Code Editor Component**

I'm currently focused on the `CodeEditor` component within `App.tsx`. My goal is to determine how to visually highlight the currently executing line during debugging. I'm examining its current implementation to identify potential modification points for this feature, and I'll keep you posted as I identify approaches.


**Pinpointing Key Component Updates**

I've been drilling down on the `CodeEditor` component's structure. I'm focusing on the component's render function, specifically how it handles lines and the gutter, in order to identify the best way to visually highlight a current line during execution. The properties `currentLine` and `lines` appear key for this task. I think a new class name could be rendered conditionally to highlight that line. I'm making progress, and will update you again shortly.


**Refining Highlighting Approach**

I've been examining the `CodeEditor` component's structure in greater detail, especially the rendering of the line numbers, and the editor area. It appears that the `currentLine` state variable is already being used to create an overlay for the current execution line. I'm considering modifications to this existing overlay to provide a more striking visual cue during debugging. I'm exploring enhancements now.


**Improving Line Highlighting**

I've realized the existing approach to line highlighting within the code editor uses absolute positioning with hardcoded heights and margins tied to line numbers, which is fragile. I'm focusing on dynamic calculations instead, especially using the `line-height` value (likely 24px via `leading-6`) from the rendered text to make sure the highlight lines up with the current line. I'll need to figure out how to derive this value dynamically to ensure accurate highlighting.


**Validating Current Line Calculation**

I've been calculating the vertical position of line highlights. Specifically, I realized the math for line positioning using the provided Tailwind classes, like `mt-4` (16px) and `leading-6` (24px line-height), appears correct. However, after that, I investigated z-index values, noting the `textarea` has a `z-index` of 10, the highlight has 0, but the container has `bg-zinc-950`. This should mean the highlight is visible, suggesting there might be an issue with `currentLine` or CSS.


**Addressing Scroll Interaction**

I've just realized the highlighting issue stems from scrolling. Specifically, the highlight div's absolute positioning is within a container that doesn't scroll with the text area. This means as the user scrolls, the highlight remains fixed in its absolute position while the text scrolls. The textarea handles its own scrolling. The gutter scrolls (synced via JS). The highlight div's `top` is calculated as `(line-1) * 24`, so I will need to use JS to dynamically update its `top` CSS value during scroll events, otherwise the line highlight will become disconnected from the current line during debug, as the user scrolls. I'm exploring solutions now.


**Integrating Scroll Updates**

I'm now focused on synchronizing the line highlight with the `textarea`'s scrolling. My main task is figuring out how to inject the highlight into a scrolling container, as the current absolute positioning within a fixed container leads to misalignment. I need to dynamically update the highlight's position based on scroll events, but I'm pondering if that is the best approach, or if the overlay needs a parent.


**Implementing Scroll Synchronization**

I've been analyzing the best way to handle scroll synchronization. It seems embedding the highlight within a scrolling container presents challenges, especially given the existing structure. I'm leaning toward using the `scrollTop` state and the existing `handleScroll` function to dynamically update the highlight's position. Adjusting the highlight logic to be scroll-aware appears to be the most promising approach for now.


**Finalizing Scroll Integration**

I'm now integrating scroll synchronization with the existing component. I've added `scrollTop` state and adjusted `handleScroll`. The highlight's `top` calculation is now `(currentLine - 1) * 24 + 16 - scrollTop`. I've confirmed that the CSS `overflow: hidden` on the container ensures the highlight stays within the visible area as the user scrolls. I believe the implementation is complete now.


**Refining Highlighting Strategy**

payload.text:
This is open router api key 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
 Add as back up api for getting error or limite corosh

**Implementing Backup API**

I'm working on integrating OpenRouter as a fallback if the Gemini API goes down or is rate limited. I've been provided with an API key, which I am now ready to integrate.


**Adding OpenRouter Fallback**

I'm now diving into modifying `services/geminiService.ts`. I'm creating a new function to interface with the OpenRouter API, using `fetch` to target the `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions` endpoint. I'm also preparing to wrap my Gemini calls in `try-catch` blocks, ready to invoke the OpenRouter fallback if Gemini encounters issues. For OpenRouter, I'm setting the `Authorization`, `Content-Type` headers, and considering `HTTP-Referer` and `X-Title`. Finally, I'm selecting OpenRouter models that are comparable to Gemini Flash and Pro.


**Selecting OpenRouter Model**

I've decided to use `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001` via OpenRouter to ensure consistency with the Gemini simulation. This is the model to focus on for this integration. I'll stick with that even when I explore other models later.


**Refining OpenRouter Interface**

I'm now honing the OpenRouter interface within `geminiService.ts`. I'm mapping complexity levels and model hints to appropriate OpenRouter models. The default for lower complexity will be `google/gemini-2.0-flash-lite-preview-02-05:free`, and I'll default to the same logic for Pro. I'm focusing on handling messages and model selection for this fallback mechanism.


**Finalizing Error Handling**

I've just added comprehensive error handling for the OpenRouter API calls within `geminiService.ts`. I'm now making sure that I handle responses that aren't 'ok', and I'm crafting specific error messages for those. My fallback code includes a `try-catch` block that reports errors from `fetch`, and if this fails, throws a new error, indicating that both the primary and fallback APIs have failed.


**Adapting System Instruction**

I've updated the error handling block in `geminiService.ts` to construct messages for OpenRouter. I'm now making sure that the `systemInstruction` from the config is correctly formatted with the necessary `{role: 'system', content: ...}` structure. I'm also ensuring the message history, if necessary, is correctly mapped for the OpenRouter call. Additionally, I am ensuring my Gemini code does not use `responseMimeType: 'application/json'`, so the fallback functions appropriately. Furthermore, the `prompt` is now adjusted to emphasize the expected JSON output, and `generateDebugTrace` is appropriately adapted.


**Integrating OpenRouter Key**

I'm now integrating the OpenRouter API key directly into `geminiService.ts`. I'm using `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` to avoid confusion with the primary API key. I'm focusing on the implementation of `callOpenRouter`, setting up the `fetch` call and body. I'm preparing to select the appropriate model, and exploring enabling JSON mode, if supported.


**Refining Fetch Request**

I'm now refining the `fetch` request within `callOpenRouter`. I've added the `method`, `headers` including `Authorization`, `Content-Type`, `HTTP-Referer`, and `X-Title`. I am also ensuring the `body` is properly stringified with the provided data. Finally, I've added error handling to check the response status and provide detailed error messages.
To implement the OpenRouter backup mechanism, I will update `services/geminiService.ts` to include a fallback function that is triggered when the primary Gemini API call fails.

entries.description: Add OpenRouter fallback mechanism for API redundancy
